coredata.dc:title: Evaluation of the laminar burning velocity of various battery vent gases emitted during the thermal runaway of Li-ion cells
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Loss Prevention in the Process Industries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Russo, Paola
Ubaldi, Sofia
coredata.dc:description: The rapid expansion of lithium-ion battery (LIB) technology across energy storage and transportation sectors raises significant safety concerns due to potential fire and explosion risks. Thermal runaway (TR) events in LIBs can release flammable gases, thereby posing heightened fire hazards. However, data on the flammability characteristics of gases emitted during thermal failure remain limited. This study addresses this gap by evaluating the laminar burning velocity (Su), a key safety parameter, using both experimental and modeling approaches to understand the influence of cell chemistry on LIB behavior. Three commercial cylindrical cells—Lithium Nickel Cobalt Aluminium Oxide (NCA), Lithium Iron Phosphate (LFP), and Lithium Nickel Manganese Cobalt Oxide (NMC)—were tested at a 100 % state of charge (SoC). Cells were subjected to controlled heating at a rate of 5 °C/min in a laboratory setup equipped with Fourier Transform-Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) and a micro-GC for real-time gas analysis. Major battery vent gas (BVG) components detected during TR event included H2, CH4, CO, CO2, HF, and vapours of electrolyte solvents like dimethyl carbonate (DMC), diethyl carbonate (DEC), and ethylene carbonate (EC). The Su values were calculated using a one-dimensional laminar premixed flame model within the CHEMKIN software, with continuous gas monitoring throughout the entire thermal failure event. These calculations considered different BVG compositions during specific phases—venting, TR, and overall event phases—each critical depending on cell chemistry. For NCA cells, the TR phase exhibited the most critical BVG composition, while for LFP and NMC cells, the venting phase proved more critical, largely due to H2 emissions. Furthermore, the effect of TR-induced temperature on Su was evaluated through simulations conducted at 25 °C, 150 °C, 300 °C, and 500 °C at 1 atm.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Thermal runaway
Battery vent gas
Laminar burning velocity
Premixed flame
CHEMKIN

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Loss Prevention in the Process Industries

This manuscript reflects only the authors' views and opinions, neither the European Union nor the European Commission can be considered responsible for them.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Cell surface temperature and internal pressure profile for thermal abuse tests on NMC cells.
Gas emissions profile for: (a) NCA; (b) LFP; (c) NMC cells.
Su calculated for BVG from NCA cells (Golubkov et al., 2015) compared with experimental data (Ueda et al., 2024) and those calculated by GRI-Mech 3.0.
Su calculated from both mechanisms for the BVG from NCA cells compared to literature data (Baird et al., 2020) (grey area).
Su, calculated from both mechanisms for the BVG from LFP cells compared to literature data (Baird et al., 2020) (grey area).
Su, calculated from both mechanisms for the BVG from NMC cells compared to literature data (Baird et al., 2020) (grey area).
Effect of temperature on maximum Su calculated for BVGs: (a) NCA; (b) LFP; (c) NMC cells (100 % SoC).
Percentage of primary emitted gases from Li-ion 18650 cells subjected to thermal abuse tests.
Chemical composition and electrical specification of Li-ion cells used in this study.

row.entry:
Anode active material: Graphite (C)
Cathode active material: Lithium nickel cobalt aluminium oxide (NCA)

Maximum operating Voltage Range:
2.75 V (0 % SoC) - 4.20 V (100 % SoC)
Rated capacity: 3200 mAh
Gravimetric Energy Density: 246 Wh/kg

Anode active material: Graphite (C)
Cathode active material: Lithium iron phosphate (LFP)

Maximum operating Voltage Range:
2.30 V (0 % SoC) – 3.70 V (100 % SoC)
Rated capacity: 1300 mAh
Gravimetric Energy Density: 104 Wh/kg

Anode active material: Graphite (C)
Cathode active material: Lithium nickel manganese cobalt oxide (NMC)

Maximum operating Voltage Range:
2.50 V (0 % SoC) - 4.20 V (100 % SoC)
Rated capacity: 2850 mAh
Gravimetric Energy Density: 209 Wh/kg

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Temperature (°C) and pressure (barg) relative to the key events of the thermal abuse tests.
Mole fractions of the gas species and relative volume (NmL) emitted during the thermal abuse test phases: venting; TR; Global.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: This article is part of a special issue entitled: 15th ISHPMIE published in Journal of Loss Prevention in the Process Industries.

head.ce:title: Evaluation of the laminar burning velocity of various battery vent gases emitted during the thermal runaway of Li-ion cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering Materials Environment, Sapienza University of Rome, Via Eudossiana 18, 00184, Rome, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering Materials Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering Materials Environment, Sapienza University of Rome, Via Eudossiana 18, 00184, Rome, Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rapid expansion of lithium-ion battery (LIB) technology across energy storage and transportation sectors raises significant safety concerns due to potential fire and explosion risks. Thermal runaway (TR) events in LIBs can release flammable gases, thereby posing heightened fire hazards. However, data on the flammability characteristics of gases emitted during thermal failure remain limited. This study addresses this gap by evaluating the laminar burning velocity (Su), a key safety parameter, using both experimental and modeling approaches to understand the influence of cell chemistry on LIB behavior. Three commercial cylindrical cells—Lithium Nickel Cobalt Aluminium Oxide (NCA), Lithium Iron Phosphate (LFP), and Lithium Nickel Manganese Cobalt Oxide (NMC)—were tested at a 100 % state of charge (SoC). Cells were subjected to controlled heating at a rate of 5 °C/min in a laboratory setup equipped with Fourier Transform-Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) and a micro-GC for real-time gas analysis. Major battery vent gas (BVG) components detected during TR event included H2, CH4, CO, CO2, HF, and vapours of electrolyte solvents like dimethyl carbonate (DMC), diethyl carbonate (DEC), and ethylene carbonate (EC).

The Su values were calculated using a one-dimensional laminar premixed flame model within the CHEMKIN software, with continuous gas monitoring throughout the entire thermal failure event. These calculations considered different BVG compositions during specific phases—venting, TR, and overall event phases—each critical depending on cell chemistry. For NCA cells, the TR phase exhibited the most critical BVG composition, while for LFP and NMC cells, the venting phase proved more critical, largely due to H2 emissions. Furthermore, the effect of TR-induced temperature on Su was evaluated through simulations conducted at 25 °C, 150 °C, 300 °C, and 500 °C at 1 atm.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The internal composition, structure and principal advantages of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have been the subject of extensive investigation and analysis in numerous academic papers (Park, 2012). Lithium-ion batteries are defined by two key characteristics: their nominal voltage range, indicated by the state of charge (SoC), and their geometry, which may be cylindrical, coin, prismatic, or pouch-shaped. The chemical composition of the internal components, such as the anode and cathode active materials or the electrolyte, is the primary chemical property (Park, 2012). The chemical compositions of cathode most commonly available on the commercial market include Lithium Nickel Cobalt Aluminium Oxide (NCA), Lithium Iron Phosphate (LFP), Lithium Nickel Manganese Cobalt Oxide (NMC), and Lithium Cobalt Oxide (LCO). With regard to the anode, two potential options exist: graphite and lithium titanate oxide (LTO). The most commonly utilised organic carbonates for the electrolyte are dimethyl carbonate (DMC), diethyl carbonate (DEC), and ethylene carbonate (EC). The safety operating windows for Li-ion cells are defined by their physical and chemical properties, expressed in terms of temperature and voltage (Zhang et al., 2018). Li-ion cells may be subjected to internal or external abuse, classified as electrical, mechanical, or thermal, outside of the specified range (Lu et al., 2013). Abuse results in the degradation of the internal components, which in turn gives rise to subsequent degradation reactions between the species. These degradations result in an increase in internal pressure and temperature, which in turn leads to venting and ultimately to thermal runaway (TR) (Lu et al., 2013). The physical and chemical properties of the Li-ion cell, in addition to the type of abuse to which the device is subjected, are directly correlated with the TR properties and the resulting products, including gases, solids, and liquids (Lopez et al., 2015; Ubaldi et al., 2023; Ubaldi and Russo, 2023b; Ubaldi and Russo, 2024b; Bugryniec et al., 2024). Furthermore, these properties contribute to the risk of fire and/or explosion.

It can be reasonably deduced that the TR is the primary cause of accidents involving electric passenger vehicles (EVs). As documented in the International Energy Agency's EV Outlook Update 2024 (IEA, 2024), there were 511 incidents of thermal runaway in electric vehicles between 2010 and June 30, 2024. It is estimated that there are approximately 40 million electric vehicles currently in operation (EV FireSafe, 2024). The category of electric vehicles encompasses both battery electric vehicles (BEVs) and plug-in hybrid electric vehicles (PHEVs). The damage to the electric vehicle battery was the primary cause of the TR, which typically resulted in a battery fire (489 instances). Nevertheless, in a minority of cases, this resulted in a vapour cloud explosion (22 occurrences). In such instances, the gases emitted accumulate and create an explosive environment, both within enclosed spaces and in open air (EV FireSafe, 2024). In terms of recent updates on this phenomenon, the latest incidents have occurred in France, with two separate occurrences of EVs exploding within a one-week period, between 16th and May 17, 2024. One incident occurred while the electric vehicle was undergoing charging, and the other while it was parked. The images and videos of the EVs and their surroundings after the explosion are noteworthy. They show, for instance, cars that have been completely uprooted and burned, as well as the collapse of part of a nearby private residence with significant structural damage (Chicheportiche, 2024). When considered alongside witness accounts, these testimonies provide a comprehensive overview of the explosion risks associated self-heating rate (SHR) with LIBs. This emphasises the necessity for a comprehensive understanding of the explosion risk, which must encompass both the chemical composition of the products emitted and the laminar burning velocity.

In terms of gas emissions, a review of the literature reveals that the gas produced during TR is primary composed of: hydrogen (H2), carbon monoxide (CO), carbon dioxide (CO2), methane (CH4), fluorinated compounds, such as hydrogen fluoride (HF) (Andersson et al., 2016; Ubaldi and Russo, 2024b), and vapours of electrolyte solvents, such as dimethyl carbonate (DMC), ethyl carbonate (EC), and diethyl carbonate (DEC) (Diaz et al., 2019; Ubaldi et al., 2023). The concentration values can be obtained through the analysis of different instrumental techniques, including Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectrometry, gas chromatography (GC) or the combination of both (Essl et al., 2020; Peng et al., 2020; Larsson et al., 2016). Table 1 presents a summary of the composition of battery vent gas (BVG) for cylindrical (18650) Li-ion cells with varying internal chemistries under different SoCs and subjected to thermal abuse testing in both inert (nitrogen, N2) or oxidative (air) environment. It is customary to present only the mean concentration of the emitted gas over the entire thermal abuse test.

As illustrated in Table 1, the primary species emitted are H2, CO2, and CO. The ratio of these species is dependent on the SoC and the chemical composition of the electrodes, and thus subject to variation. The percentage of hydrogen is the lowest for LTO (8.4 % at 100 % SoC), with a range of 10 % (LCO/NMC at 100 % SoC) to 38.3 % (LCO at 50 % SoC) for the other cell chemistries. The highest levels of CO were emitted by the NCA (48.9 % at 100 % SoC) and NMC (30.3 % at 100 % SoC) cells, while the highest percentage of CO2 (66.2 % at 50 % SoC and 53.0 mol % at 100 % SoC) was emitted by the LFP cell. The composition of BVG has been measured in a variety of experimental conditions, including the type of feed gas and equipment used (e.g., accelerating rate calorimeter (ARC) or reactor chamber) and the coupled analytical instruments (e.g., GC or FT-IR).

The papers under consideration lack information regarding the electrolyte and fluorinated compounds, including DMC, DEC, EC, and HF, that are released during the TR. The aforementioned species present in the BVG have the potential to contribute to the flammability and/or toxicity of the gas. With regard to the toxicity of the gas, the literature (Peng et al., 2020; Ubaldi and Russo, 2024a,b) suggests that HF and CO may reach the Immediately Dangerous to Life and Health (IDLH) limits, as defined by the National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health (NIOSH, 2023).

With regard to flammability, the vented gases have the potential to ignite either within or outside the battery (accumulating into a module). This depends depending on a number of factors, including temperature, pressure, flow velocity, and the composition of the gas mixture. In order to evaluate the fire and explosion risk associated with BVG, it is essential to assess key gas properties, including the lower flammability limit (LFL), laminar burning velocity (Su), and the maximum explosion overpressure. Su represents a fundamental combustion property and a crucial parameter for understanding flame propagation, gas explosions, and combustion reaction mechanisms (Henriksen et al., 2021a, 2022). The two principal methods for obtaining Su are experiment and one-dimensional (1-D) modelling. The measurements are conducted using a variety of methods, including the use of constant pressure and volume combustion chambers with the spherical flame method, particle tracking velocimetry, cylindrical bombs, the burner method with conical flames, and heat flux burners. Moreover, it can be derived through 1-D simulations utilising software such as Cantera (Henriksen et al., 2021b; Baird et al., 2020 ), Chemkin (Ma et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2023), and Opensmoke++ (Cuoci et al., 2015; OpenSMOKE, 2024). The aforementioned simulations employ chemical kinetic mechanisms for the calculation of Su. The accuracy of the simulations is contingent upon the size, rigidity, and numerical configuration of the mechanism employed.

Despite the critical importance of safety in battery vent gases (BVG) from lithium-ion batteries, few published studies focus on evaluating the laminar burning velocity of these gases. Some modelling studies (Baird et al., 2020; Fernandes et al., 2018) rely on experimental data from other authors, though their analyses are constrained by the use of chemical mechanisms with limited capacity. Henriksen et al. (2021b) assessed the laminar burning velocities of typical Li-ion vent gas mixtures, but excluded BVG containing carbonates and fluorinated compounds, aiming instead to create gas mixtures that are representative and suitable for precise experimental and modelling studies. However, fluorinated species have been shown to influence Su and explosion characteristics of mixtures, as demonstrated in Gao et al. (2021) through their research in hydrogen explosion suppression.

The Su value for BVG has been found to depend on both the state of charge and the cell's chemical composition. Baird et al. (2020) observed that Su values increase with higher SoC, while Henriksen et al. (2021b) reported that NCA cells exhibited a maximum Su (1.0 m/s), higher than that of LCO (0.6 m/s) and LFP cells (0.3 m/s). This behaviour is largely attributable to the BVG composition: NCA cells release gases with higher hydrogen and lower carbon dioxide content compared to other cells. Additionally, the peak of Su occurs at an equivalent ratio (ER) of around 1.0 across all cell types and SoC levels.

This study uniquely integrates experimental and modelling approaches to evaluate the laminar burning velocity (Su) for different Li-ion chemistries, using the composition of battery vent gases (BVG) quantified through standardized thermal abuse testing. Initially, thermal abuse tests were conducted on Li-ion cells to both identify gas components and to measure their concentrations. Subsequently, the Su values were calculated based on these composition data.

The tests pertain to 18650 Li-ion cells with three distinct chemistries, namely NCA, LFP, and NMC, at an identical SoC of 100 %. The cells were subjected to a thermal abuse test in a laboratory apparatus with a heating rate of 5 °C/min and air flow. The evaluated compounds included H2, CO, CO2, CH4, and fluorinated compounds such as HF, as well as vapours of electrolyte solvents, specifically DMC, EC, and DEC. The Su was evaluated using a 1-D premixed laminar flame model of the CHEMKIN Premix module. According to Nilsson et al. (2023), a chemical kinetic mechanism that incorporates common hydrocarbon compounds in vent gases (i.e. three carbonates, and several fluorinated compounds) was constructed. The presented mechanism has been validated and is employed to enhance comprehension of the combustion characteristics of Li-ion BVG compositions, including all classes of molecular components that are commonly present. The calculation of Su was performed by taking into account the BVG composition of the various phases of the thermal abuse test, namely venting, TR, and the global event.

In order to evaluate the thermal behaviour of 18650 Li-ion cells with varying chemical compositions of cathode, thermal abuse tests were conducted in a laboratory apparatus. During the tests temperature, pressure and composition of the emitted gases were monitored. Subsequently, the experimental identification and quantification of the BVGs were employed to evaluate the Su of the BVG emitted.

Table 2 presents the chemical composition of the cylindrical Li-ion cells (18650) that were considered in this study. The chemical information was obtained from the material safety data sheet (MSDS), while the electrical specifications were reported in the technical data sheet (TDS) of the respective Li-ion cell. Each Li-ion cell is identified with an identification code (Id-cell), which corresponds with the cathode active material.

Prior to testing, the Li-ion cells were subjected to a standard procedure of five charge–discharge cycles to ensure the correct formation of the solid electrolyte interface (SEI). The procedure was conducted using the Battery Test System BaSyTec CTS, produced by Thasar (Italy). The cells were initially discharged at a C/2 discharge current, resulting in a fully discharged state corresponding to the minimum operating voltage value, designated as 0 % SoC. Subsequently, an interval of 1 h was permitted for the cell to stabilise its state. Thereafter, a primary phase of charge at a constant current (CC) of C/2 was initiated until the maximum operating voltage value was reached, designated as 100 % SoC. Subsequently, a second phase of charge is applied at constant voltage (CV), with the current decreasing until it reaches a value of 0.5 A. After allowing the cell to stabilise for a further 1-h interval, the cycle is repeated four additional times.

Upon completion of the five charge–discharge cycles, a 100 % SoC was achieved, thereby applying the same charging phase that was applied during the SEI formation process. The maximum operating voltage range is directly correlated with the SoC of the Li-ion cells, as indicated in the technical data sheets outlined in Table 2. It should be noted that the maximum voltage permitted for NCA and NMC batteries is 4.20 V, while for LFP batteries it is 3.70 V.

The laboratory apparatus was set up to conduct thermal abuse tests with an inlet airflow of 500 NmL/min over a temperature range of 20–500 °C. The heating rate was 5 °C/min in air. Upon reaching the TR condition, the oven was switched off. Two thermocouples, designated TC1 and TC2, were positioned on the surface of the cell in order to monitor the temperature of the cell. Furthermore, a pressure transducer was positioned within the reactor in order to monitor any changes in pressure.

The gases emitted by the lithium-ion cell during the thermal abuse test inside the reactor chamber were purged by a controlled draft system. The gases were separated into two distinct streams, with one directed to the FT-IR gas analyser and the other to the micro-GC. The initial gas stream was subjected to filtration in order to remove any particulate matter, after which it was connected to a transfer line leading to the FT-IR gas analyser. In order to prevent condensation of the vapours, the filter unit, transfer line and FT-IR gas analysis cell are maintained at a temperature of 180 °C. The second gas stream, passing through another transfer line, establishes a connection between the reactor chamber outlet and the micro-GC. In this instance, a trap comprising sodium hydroxide (NaOH in H2O 1.5 M) is situated before the injection port of the micro-GC. The purpose of the trap is to remove fluorinated compounds by precipitation, thereby protecting the column from corrosion.

The online FT-IR spectrometer (Spectrum 3, PerkinElmer) was employed to collect spectra with a mercury cadmium telluride (MCT) detector. The spectrometric parameters were as follows: resolution of 4 cm−1 in the spectral range between 4500 cm−1 and 650 cm−1, with a scan/spectrum of 8. Continuous acquisition was performed using the Time Base tool of the Spectrum IR software (PerkinElmer), while identification and quantification were conducted through the use of standard spectra and calibration lines, employing the SpectrumQuant software (PerkinElmer). The standard spectra and relative calibration lines for each species (CO, HF, DMC, DEC and EC) were obtained through the utilisation of technical gases. From the individual spectra of the pure gaseous species, the primary wave numbers were identified and expressed in cm−1. Subsequently, the standard spectra were obtained at varying concentrations in order to construct the requisite calibration line utilising the SpectrumQuant software (PerkinElmer). As the FT-IR technique is unable to detect bimolecular species such as H2, a micro-GC (990, Agilent) was also employed.

The continuous micro-GC was employed for the hydrogen measurements. A PoraPLOT U column was coupled with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD). The injector was maintained at a temperature of 90 °C, and the oven was set to the same temperature. The acquisition mode was in continuous, with an injection time of 10 s. Identification and quantification were obtained using standard chromatograms and the relative calibration lines obtained using Soprane CSD software (SRA).

By quantifying the different species in real time, throughout the duration of the thermal abuse test, it is obtained the concentration profile for each species over time. The total concentration emitted over the specified time period was obtained by integrating the area under the curve for each gas. In the present study, three BVG compositions were considered relevant to the three phases: (i) venting, from the commencement of venting to the onset of thermal runaway; (ii) TR, from the commencement of venting to the peak temperature; and (iii) global, encompassing the entire event.
For each chemical composition (NCA, LFP, and NMC), the Li-ion cells were subjected to three thermal abuse tests.
A detailed description of the reactor, acquisition and quantification procedure can be found in a previous study (Ubaldi et al., 2023).

A one-dimensional flame propagation model of the CHEMKIN Premix module was implemented to for the purpose of evaluating the Su for the various BVG compositions. The study employed a comprehensive reaction mechanism to simulate the combustion of the BVG.

A review of the existing literature reveals that no chemical kinetics models encompass all the distints fuel types present in the Li-ion BVG. The chemical set proposed by Nilsson et al. (2023) comprises the kinetics mechanism for the following species: DMC (as proposed by Alexandrino et al., 2018), DEC (as proposed by Sun et al., 2017), F-compounds (as proposed by Linteris and Babushok, 2020), C1-C2 (as proposed by Metcalfe et al., 2013), and H2 (as proposed by Kéromnès et al., 2013). Moreover, the EC chemistry set proposed by Takahashi et al. (2022) was also taken into account. The authors of the considered kinetics mechanisms have confirmed their validity through a comparison with experimental trends of the individual components. Moreover, Nilsson et al. (2023) illustrated that the proposed mechanism is appropriate for modelling typical Li-ion BVG compositions.

A validation study was conducted by comparing simulated laminar burning velocities with experimental measurements (Ueda et al., 2024) for NCA vent gas compositions (50–127 % SoC; Golubkov et al., 2015). Additional comparisons with the GRI-Mech 3.0 mechanism are also provided.

A one-dimensional laminar premixed flame was simulated by integrating chemical reaction kinetics, thermal properties, and transport properties. To characterize all the species present in the BVG obtained from the experimental thermal abuse tests, this study employs a novel chemical set, as described in section 2.1. A hybrid time-integration and Newton-iteration method was applied to resolve the steady-state mass, species, and energy conservation equations. Transport properties were evaluated using mixture-averaged formulations. Initial and boundary conditions were established to define the environmental parameters of the problem. In the simulations, the curvature and gradient parameters were initially set to 0.2, resulting in a computational grid of 300 points. To ensure an accurate solution, three continuation steps were conducted, with decreasing curvature and gradient parameters at each step (0.8, 0.5, and 0.2, respectively). The unburned mixture was introduced at a fixed inlet velocity of 40.0 cm/s and at variable temperatures (25 °C, 150 °C, 300 °C, and 500 °C), with a pressure of 1 atm and a specified inlet composition. At the exit boundary, all gradients were defined to vanish.

Fig. 1 illustrates the typical logarithmic profile of temperature (monitored by thermocouples TC1 and TC2 placed on the cell surface) and internal reactor pressure, which reflect venting, the onset of TR, and the maximum peak observed during thermal abuse tests on NMC cells.

The graph of Fig. 1, along with previous graphs for NCA and LFP cells, reported in Ubaldi and Russo (2024b), presents both the cell surface temperature and the internal reactor pressure, which rises due to the gas released during the thermal abuse test.

A primary observation from the these tests is that the temperature and pressure values reached in NCA and NMC cells tests are higher than those in the LFP cells tests, although all exhibit a similar trend. At the beginning of the heating phase, temperature increases, while pressure remains almost constant. Subsequently, pressure rises while temperature declines slightly due to the gas venting through the venting valve. This initial pressure increase is attributed to gas release from the cell within the reactor, while the temperature drop is due to the cooling effect of electrolyte evaporation as it escapes the cell. A sharp increase in both temperature and pressure follows, marking the onset of the TR, which continues until the maximum pressure and temperature peaks are reached. The onset of TR is characterized by a self-heating rate (SHR) at which the temperature increase exceeds 10 °C/min, in accordance with the criteria established by Doughty and Roth (2012).

Table 3 presents a summary of key results, detailing the temperature at the cell surface, and internal reactor pressure at critical events - namely, venting, onset of thermal runaway, and the maximum peak - observed across the three thermal abuse tests conducted for each cell chemistry.

A primary distinction emerges in the venting temperatures: it is 154 °C for NCA, 183 °C for NMC, and 205 °C for LFP cells. The most notable discrepancy lies in the maximum temperature reached, with NCA attaining approximately 562 °C, NMC exceeding 575 °C and LFP approximately 358 °C. Both NCA and NMC cells exhibit similar maximum pressure values, surpassing 4 barg, while LFP cells show a lower peak pressure, under 2 barg.

These maximum temperatures were further compared with values reported in the literature. Golubkov et al. (2014, 2015) documented a peak temperature of 911 °C for NCA and 404 °C and 678 °C for NMC cells, while Yuan et al. (2020) reported 917 °C for NMC cells. It is crucial to recognize that factors such as cell characteristics (e.g., chemistry and voltage), and experimental conditions (e.g., feed gas and heating rate) significantly influence the observed maximum temperatures. Defining cell characteristics solely based on cathode composition is insufficient, as variations in anode and electrolyte composition, which may depend on the manufacturer, also play a role. For instance, differences in the type and ratios of solvents (e.g., DEC, DMC, and EC) used in the electrolyte are often omitted from the material safety data sheet. Additionally, voltage ranges may vary according to cell's intended application, meaning a 100 % state of charge does not correspond to a single, fixed voltage, as indicated in Table 2.

Fig. 2 presents the gas emission profiles of H2, CO, CO2, CH4, EC, DMC, and HF throughout the thermal abuse tests for NCA, LFP and NMC cells. the DEC quantification is not included in Fig. 2, as it remained null across all tests.

The concentration profiles in Fig. 2 show trends similar to the temperature and pressure profiles observed in Fig. 1, reflecting key events such as venting, onset of thermal runaway, and maximum peaks. Notably, maximum peak concentrations of CO, CO2 and H2 in both NCA and NMC cells were in the range 2.0–2.8 × 105 ppmv, while CH4 reached a peak around 0.5 × 105 ppmv. Electrolyte-related compounds, DMC and EC, reached peak concentrations of approximately 103 ppmv. The three emission profiles differ most significantly in H2 release patterns: for NCA cells, H2 is released near the maximum temperature, whereas for LFP and NMC cells, H2 emission begins earlier, during venting, with a significant amount released at the onset of TR.

These differences can be linked to the thermal stability of the electrodes, as the decomposition reactions in Li-ion cells during TR correlate closely with the internal component composition. For instance, the SEI layer degradation on the graphite anode begins around 80–120 °C, following the reaction:
(1)
(
CH 2
OCO 2
Li )
2
→
Li 2
CO 3
+
C 2
H 4
+
CO 2
+ 0.5
O 2

The cathode materials’ varying thermal stability and decomposition reactions also affect the emissions profile. Based on the literature, cathode materials can be ordered by increasing thermal stability: LFP is the most stable, followed by NMC, with NCA being the least stable (Feng et al., 2018). For example, the LFP cathode remains stable up to approximately 310 °C due to strong P=O covalent bonds (Jiang and Dahn, 2004). Its degradation, upon reaching the onset temperature, follows equation (2):
(2)
2
LiFePO 4
→
Fe 2
P 2
O 7
+ 0.5
O 2

NMC decomposition varies with the Ni, Co, and Mn ratio, with NMC 111 degrading from approximately 260 °C according to equation (3) (Belharouak et al., 2003):
(3)
Li 0.35
( NiCoMn )
1 / 3
O 2
→
Li 0.35
( NiCoMn )
1 / 3
O
2 − y
+ y / 2
O 2

NCA degradation initiates at 160 °C, proceeding as follows (Wang et al., 2007):
(4)
Li 0.36
Ni 0.8
Co 0.1
Al 0.05
O 2
→
0.18
Li 2
O 2
+ 0.8
NiO + 0.05
Co 3
O 4
+ 0.025
Al 2
O 3
+ 0.372
O 2

The differences in emission profiles, shown in Fig. 2, are thus attributed to the temperatures at which venting occurs. For NCA, venting aligns with cathode degradation onset, leading to the release of CO and CO2, while in LFP and NMC cells, venting temperatures remain below the cathode decomposition threshold, releasing only electrolyte and F-compounds.

Additional considerations can be made regarding gas production and consumption. For instance, DMC is generally detected during testing, while EC emissions are linked to temperatures exceeding 248 °C due to EC's higher boiling point, suggesting it only vaporizes after TR onset (Bresser et al., 2015). After reaching the maximum temperature, all emissions decline to ambient levels except for EC.

The analysis of the CO to CO2 ratio provides further insight into both the extent of combustion and the combustion characteristics of the electrolyte components. The combustion properties of specific electrolyte components also influenced gas production: the combustion of volatile electrolytes like DMC and DEC predominantly yields CO2 around 200 °C, whereas EC - a high-boiling component - produces CO at elevated temperatures between 400 and 500 °C due to its higher boiling point (Zhang et al., 2014). This variation underscores how electrolyte composition and thermal stability impact combustion products across different battery chemistries.

In terms of combustion completeness, CO production observed in all cells except LFP indicates partial combustion, likely due to the large quantity of emitted products that remain unreacted even in an oxygen-rich environment. In contrast, LFP's stability up to 310 °C results in fewer emissions and near-complete combustion, leaving no unburned products.
Table 4 presents the mole fractions and relative gas volumes, in NmL, across different phases of the thermal abuse test, namely venting, thermal runaway (TR), and the entire event (global).

As depicted in Fig. 2 and discussed earlier, the primary variation in gas concentration profiles among phases is the presence of H2. Over the entire test duration, NCA exhibited 23.6 % H2, predominantly released during the TR phase and absent during venting. During the venting phase, CO, CO2, CH4, and HF concentrations remained steady, whereas CH4 levels rose during TR compared to CO, CO2, and HF. Of the species attributed to the electrolyte, DMC appeared throughout the test, whereas EC was only detected in the TR phase, with each maintaining percentages below 1.0 %.

In contrast, the gas emission profiles of LFP and NMC revealed distinct patterns. For LFP, H2 concentrations increased progressively from the venting phase, forming a bell-shaped profile not seen in other chemistries. Other species followed similar trends, with lower electrolyte-species emissions from the venting phase and notable CO and CO2 increases post-TR. NMC exhibited an elevated H2 concentration in the venting phase (83.9 %) compared to TR (39.5 %) due to substantial CH4, CO, and CO2 emissions occurring primarily during TR. Among the electrolyte species, DMC was detected only during venting, and EC only during TR, likely due to their differing boiling points (90 °C for DMC and 244 °C for EC).

For comparative purposes, the global BVG data from this study were aligned with literature findings, as shown in Table 1. When comparing the NCA BVG from this study with Golubkov et al. (2015) on NCA at 100 % SoC tested in N2, similar H2 levels were observed (23.6 % vs. 25.6 %), though CO2 levels differed (39.5 % vs. 20 %), likely due to variations in atmospheric conditions during testing. For LFP, Golubkov et al. (2014, 2015) reported higher H2 (29.4 % vs. 18.4 %) and CO2 (48.3 % vs. 37.9 %) but lower CO levels (9.1 % vs. 37.1 %). For NMC, Yuan et al. (2020) reported lower H2 (12.5 % vs. 40.7 %) and CO2 (19.9 % vs. 39.2 %) but higher CO (28.1 % vs. 14.0 %), likely influenced by variances in cell composition, testing conditions, and analytical techniques employed.

The emitted gas volume per phase is also critical. NCA, LFP, and NMC released 1.27 × 104 NmL, 1.96 × 104 NmL, and 3.12 × 104 NmL of gas, respectively, across the entire test. During venting and TR phases, NCA produced approximately 103 NmL, while LFP and NMC emitted around 102 NmL, reflecting the high CO and CO2 emissions from NCA during venting. These values underscore the influence of cell chemistry on gas emission profiles during thermal abuse conditions.

In order to validate the proposed mechanism, the calculated laminar burning velocities were compared with experimental measurements by Ueda et al. (2024) for NCA cell vent gas compositions at varying states of charge (SoC: 50 %, 100 %, and 127 %) as reported by Golubkov et al. (2015). To further facilitate comparison, the laminar burning velocities calculated using the GRI-Mech 3.0 mechanism are also reported in Fig. 3 . It should be noted that the GRI-Mech 3.0 mechanism does not encompass the kinetics of the electrolyte carbonates. The calculated Su values demonstrate good agreement with experimental results, as evidenced by the average error calculation, which yielded 9.7 % for GRI-Mech 3.0 and 7.7 % for the mechanism proposed in this study. This observation underscores the efficacy of the proposed mechanism in predicting the laminar burning velocity of battery vent gas.

The following section reports the calculated burning velocities obtained from both mechanisms, using GRI-Mech 3.0 as the benchmark for comparison. Fig. 4 presents the Su vs. ER plot for NCA cells, under standard conditions (25 °C, 1 atm) calculated for the three BVG compositions (venting, TR, and global) corresponding to the thermal abuse test phases. The results show that the highest Su value (45.5 cm/s) occurs when considering the global composition over the entire event, followed by 44.4 cm/s in the TR phase and 32.5 cm/s in the venting phase. These differences arise from variations in the H2 mole fractions across phases, with higher H2 concentration during the TR and global phases leading to elevated Su values compared to the venting phase. Additionally, the global BVG composition exhibits a bell-shaped Su curve between ER values of 0.4 and 2.2, while TR BVG exhibits it between 0.4 and 3.0, a trend not observed in the venting phase. This behavior is attributed to the lower flammability of gases released during the venting phase.

When compared to GRI-Mech 3.0, the model proposed in this work generally predicts lower Su values, as previously noted in Fig. 3. However, an exception to this is the venting phase, where both H2 and CH4 concentrations fall below their lower flammability limit, resulting in lower predicted values from GRI-Mech 3.0.

Fig. 5 shows the Su vs. ER plot for LFP cells at 100 % SoC, also under standard conditions (25 °C and 1 atm). In this case, the TR BVG composition yields the highest Su (105.9 cm/s), followed by the venting BVG (88.2 cm/s) and the global BVG (42.0 cm/s). These variations are found to be correlated with the H2 mole fraction, as this species is released at the time of TR onset, driving the significantly higher Su in the TR phase. The subsequent increase in CO2 mole fraction from TR onset to its peak tempers the global Su. Notably, all LFP BVG compositions exhibit a bell-shaped Su curve within the ER range of 0.5–2.4, suggesting consistent flammability characteristics. The Su calculated with the two mechanisms yielded similar results. However, greater differences are again evident for the venting phase, presumably attributable to the higher concentrations of HF and carbonates that are not considered in the GRI-Mech 3.0 mechanism.

Finally, Fig. 6 presents the Su vs. ER plot for NMC cells at 100 % SoC (25 °C and 1 atm). While similar trends are expected, differences in Su profiles may arise due to the distinct gas compositions and thermal stability of NMC materials under the tested conditions.

In Fig. 6, the effect of H2 on Su values becomes apparent, particularly as the venting phase, with its high H2 content, yields significantly higher Su values (222.6 cm/s) than the TR phase (77.2 cm/s) and global event (59.9 cm/s). This decline in Su in later phases aligns with the increase in CO2 and decrease in H2 percentages as the thermal abuse test progresses. In this case, the results of the two mechanisms are closely aligned.

In their study, Baird et al. (2020) analysed Su variability across various cathode chemistries (NCA, LFP, LCO, and NMC) at 100 % SoC, examining Su versus ER in the 0.6–1.5 ER range. In Figs. 4–6 the Su values calculated by Baird et al. (2020) are compared with those calculated in this study for NCA, LFP, and NMC cells, respectively. The results reveal distinct Su profiles for different chemistries: NCA cells show the highest maximum Su (105.0 cm/s), followed by NMC (62.8 cm/s) and LFP (38.6 cm/s) cells. Furthermore, NCA cells exhibit a broader Su range compared to the narrower ranges seen for LFP and NMC cells. The Su values obtained for NCA in this study fall within Baird's range, while only the global BVG curves for LFP and NMC align with Baird's range; in venting and TR phase, Su values are higher. This discrepancy underscores the dynamic nature of BVG emissions, which vary across phases and are not uniformly released throughout the thermal event.

It is important to emphasise that the Baird et al. (2020) data refer to different cell types (prismatic, pouch, 18650) with different electrolytes and under different test conditions. Notably, these reference tests differ from the present work in two key aspects: i) thermal abuse was conducted in inert atmospheres (N2 or Ar) rather than air, and ii) gas analysis was performed post-thermal runaway rather than throughout all phases.

Additionally, the BVG gases are emitted at elevated temperatures rather than ambient conditions, as indicated in Table 3. To assess temperature effects, Su was calculated at varying temperatures (25, 150, 300, and 500 °C) and at 1 atm. Fig. 7 presents the maximum Su values across different BVG compositions and temperatures. Experimental data on the laminar burning rate of BVG at non-standard temperatures are scarce in the literature, warranting further investigation.

Fig. 7 demonstrates that increasing temperature significantly enhances Su across all BVG compositions, with notable differences between chemistries. NCA cells show relatively consistent Su values across BVG compositions, peaking at 345 cm/s for the global BVG at 500°C. In contrast, LFP cells attain a higher maximum Su (735 cm/s, at 500°C) in the TR phase, while NMC cells reach an intermediate Su (574 cm/s) in the same phase. These findings highlight the influence of BVG composition and temperature on Su, emphasizing the need for phase-specific gas analysis in battery safety assessments.

This study undertakes a comprehensive assessment of the flammability risks associated with gases emitted from Li-ion cells during thermal runaway events. Specifically, it investigates the laminar burning velocity of gases released by various cell chemistries, namely Nickel Cobalt Aluminum Oxide (NCA), Lithium Iron Phosphate (LFP), and Nickel Manganese Cobalt Oxide (NMC) cells. By analysing experimental data on the battery venting gas composition obtained from thermal abuse tests conducted in air at a 100 % state of charge, it derives insights into the behaviour of each cell type under extreme conditions. The kinetic model employed incorporates relevant gas component types to effectively predict both the combustion of hydrocarbons and the formation of toxic fluorinated compounds during the TR process. The model has been validated using experimental data available in the literature and well-validated models, such as GRI-Mech 3.0.

The thermal abuse test results reveal that cell chemistry significantly impacts the maximum temperature reached during thermal runaway events. For instance, the peak temperatures for NCA, LFP, and NMC cells were observed to be 562 ± 96 °C, 358 ± 70 °C, and 575 ± 101 °C, respectively. Additionally, the composition of the BVG changes based on the stage of the thermal event, with notable differences between the venting phase and the TR onset phase. During a TR event in NCA cells, the venting phase releases gas mixtures rich in carbon monoxide (CO), electrolyte solvent and carbon dioxide (CO2). The highest Su for NCA cells is observed with the global BVG composition, suggesting that the specific composition of gases influences the burning characteristics.

In contrast, both LFP and NMC cells release gases with high concentrations of hydrogen (H2) at different phases. For LFP cells, the elevated Su is associated with the TR phase, whereas, for NMC cells, it occurs primarily during venting. This variance underscores the importance of distinguishing between the specific BVG compositions at each stage of thermal abuse. Comparing the Su curves from each phase with existing literature further highlights this necessity, as significant discrepancies emerge between the observed Su maxima and those found in studies based on averaged BVG compositions without distinguishing phases.

Temperature effects on Su were also investigated, with BVG temperatures ranging from 150 to 500 °C. As anticipated, an increase in temperature resulted in higher Su values, which maintained statistically significant correlations across the temperature range studied. This finding reinforces the impact of temperature on flammability and combustion behaviour, providing essential data for safety assessments in scenarios involving thermal abuse of Li-ion cells.
Paola Russo: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Sofia Ubaldi: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Paola Russo reports financial support was provided by Ministry of University and Research. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was carried out within the MOST – Sustainable Mobility Center and received funding from the European Union Next-GenerationEU (PIANO NAZIONALE DI RIPRESA E RESILIENZA (PNRR) – MISSIONE 4 COMPONENTE 2, INVESTIMENTO 1.4 – D.D. 1033 June 17, 2022, CN00000023). This manuscript reflects only the authors' views and opinions, neither the European Union nor the European Commission can be considered responsible for them.
